reasoning: Authentication operates on a claimed identity. You cannot authenticate (verify) something you have not first identified — authentication tools need an identifier to compare credentials against. From a project-sequencing perspective, identification work (account inventory, naming convention, service account cleanup) typically must be in place before authentication enforcement can be designed and tested.

Identifier reuse prevention only matters if identifiers are uniquely associated with users, processes, or devices in the first place. IA.L1-3.5.1 establishes that uniqueness; IA.L2-3.5.5 then governs how long that uniqueness must be preserved before an identifier can be assigned to someone or something new.

Disabling inactive identifiers requires having identifiers in the first place, with last-use tracking. IA.L1-3.5.1 provides the unique identifiers; IA.L2-3.5.6 then governs their lifecycle when they fall out of use. Both are typically managed through the same identity provider feature set.

MFA is an enhancement of basic authentication, not a replacement. The Assessment Guide explicitly notes that IA.L2-3.5.3 enhances IA.L1-3.5.2 — MFA adds factors on top of the basic single-factor authentication that IA.L1-3.5.2 establishes. You cannot enforce MFA on authentication that does not yet exist.

Replay-resistant authentication is a property of the authentication mechanism itself. IA.L1-3.5.2 establishes that authentication occurs; IA.L2-3.5.4 then constrains the protocols used for that authentication to ones that resist replay. Choosing modern protocols (Kerberos, TLS-protected, FIDO2) for IA.L1-3.5.2 satisfies IA.L2-3.5.4 by default.

Password complexity is a strengthening of the basic password authentication that IA.L1-3.5.2 establishes. A complexity policy without enforced authentication has nothing to apply to; basic authentication without a meaningful complexity policy is technically present but cryptographically weak. The two practices are typically configured together in the same IDP password policy.

Password reuse prevention reinforces the basic password authentication established by IA.L1-3.5.2. Without history depth enforcement, users cycle through near-identical passwords and effectively defeat any rotation requirements — which weakens the underlying authentication. Configured in the same IDP password policy as IA.L1-3.5.2.

Forced change of temporary passwords reinforces the trust model of IA.L1-3.5.2's authentication enforcement. Temporary passwords often follow predictable patterns and are issued through less secure channels — leaving them in place indefinitely undermines the authentication strength the broader policy is trying to establish.

Cryptographic protection of passwords in storage and transit is what makes the authentication required by IA.L1-3.5.2 actually safe. Authentication that occurs over plaintext channels or against plaintext-stored passwords technically satisfies 'authentication exists' but provides essentially no security against credential interception or database compromise.

Obscuring authentication input prevents shoulder-surfing capture of the credentials that IA.L1-3.5.2's authentication relies on. Most modern systems handle this by default, but custom or legacy applications with unmasked password fields directly weaken the authentication process they support.

Replay resistance and MFA reinforce each other for network access. The Assessment Guide notes that MFA itself confers some replay resistance; conversely, replay-resistant protocols make MFA harder to bypass even if individual factors are captured. The two practices are typically satisfied by the same modern authentication stack — TLS-protected MFA challenges with one-time codes or FIDO2 assertions.
